data.title: CICC: The Financial Moment of AI
data.description:
Authors: Lin Yingqi, Che Shuyun, et al.; Source: CICC Insights

AI capital expenditure is at a turning point, shifting from being driven by internal cash flow to expanding the balance sheet. As investment scale exceeds the carrying capacity of internal cash flow, marginal funding will increasingly come from the financial system. This report, the first in the "AI Financing Tracking" series, attempts to answer three questions: How will the financial system absorb the $3.5 trillion AI financing demand? Can AI investment meet debt repayment and equity returns? What opportunities will this round of AI financing cycle bring to financial institutions, and what risks will it accumulate and transmit?

Abstract

AI infrastructure capital expenditure: From cash flow to debt. The ratio of capital expenditure to revenue for large cloud vendors rose from approximately 12% in 2023 to approximately 23% in 2025. According to market consensus expectations, this ratio may further rise to over 40% in 2027, approaching or even exceeding the historical highs of capital expenditure cycles in the internet and energy industries.

Under pressure from AI capital expenditures, cloud vendors' AI investments may shift from operating cash flow to greater reliance on external financing. Based on market consensus expectations for operating cash flow and capital expenditures of major cloud vendors, we estimate that AI capital expenditures are expected to generate approximately $3.5 trillion in external financing needs over the next five years. Where will this $3.5 trillion come from? In the baseline scenario, we expect that the $3.5 trillion in external financing for AI capital expenditures will be absorbed by public market equity ($0.4 trillion), investment-grade bonds ($1.5 trillion), leveraged financing ($0.3 trillion), asset-backed securities ($0.3 trillion), and private equity ($1.1 trillion). Investment-grade bonds and private equity are the ballast for financing; the former relies on the expansion of cloud vendors' balance sheets and their cash flow repayment capabilities, while the latter can meet the financing needs of higher-risk, larger-scale projects and can also reduce cloud vendors' capital investment through off-balance-sheet financing structures, allowing for more flexible supplementation of financing gaps. A trillion-dollar question: How will AI debt be repaid? To meet debt repayment needs and shareholder returns, assuming a ROIC of 10%, we estimate that AI applications will ultimately need to generate approximately $1 trillion in sustainable revenue annually. Assuming this revenue scale is reached by 2030, this means AI application revenue needs to nearly double annually over the next five years. More important than revenue scale are profit margins and asset lifespan: at a mature EBITDA profit margin of approximately 50%, infrastructure depreciation periods of approximately 5 years are needed for investments to potentially yield positive returns; if the profit margin is below 20%, even with a longer asset lifespan, it will be difficult to cover capital costs. Can refinancing replace cash flow? Large cloud vendors typically issue long-term bonds with maturities of 10-30 years, and infrastructure funds often have investment periods of 10 years or more; after data centers are operational, construction period financing can be replaced through project bonds and securitization. However, refinancing can only buy time, not replace cash flow: if project utilization, profit margins, and asset lifespan are consistently lower than expected, continuous rolling financing will only increase leverage and financing costs. Our calculations indicate that the peak maturity period for bonds issued by large cloud vendors will be from 2027 to 2032, with an annual maturity scale of approximately $28 billion, a 60% increase compared to 2024-2026, leading to increased refinancing pressure. Opportunities and Risks for Financial Institutions. Banks can generate revenue from equity and debt underwriting, trading, M&A advisory, and project financing, while private equity and insurance institutions gain new long-term assets. In the second quarter of 2026, the non-interest income of the six largest US banks increased by 32% year-on-year; as of June 2026, bank loans to non-bank financial institutions increased by approximately 25% year-on-year, with AI financing being a significant contributor to this growth. Bank revenue is typically recognized during the financing and construction phases, while credit risk only becomes apparent during project commissioning and refinancing, exhibiting a "revenue upfront, risk down" characteristic. Is AI a Financial "Bubble"? Currently, AI investment is primarily driven by large cloud vendors with strong cash flow, and equity and subordinated capital can absorb losses before the senior tranche of bank loans. Therefore, even if some projects fail to meet expectations, the risk is more likely to manifest first as valuation adjustments for related companies, a slowdown in capital expenditure, and localized credit losses, rather than immediately escalating into a financial system crisis. On the other hand, the rapid growth of external financing, particularly private equity and leveraged financing, has also created financial spillover effects. If commercialization growth continues to lag behind capital expenditure, the risk will gradually shift from valuation corrections to credit quality deterioration, and will be transmitted through channels such as revolving financing, private lending, and securitization within the financial system. The scale of financing itself is not the problem; the real test is whether a cash flow cycle can be established before financing costs rise and assets depreciate.

Chart 1: Where did the $3.5 trillion in AI financing come from?

...

Note: AI capital expenditure and OCF support are based on market consensus expectations; 10% equity ratio in the public market is assumed; the scale of investment-grade bond issuance is calculated based on the debt ratings, leverage ratios, and bond concentration constraints of large cloud vendors; leveraged financing includes high-yield bonds and leveraged loans, and securitized assets include ABS and CMBS, estimated considering market capacity and acceptance; gaps other than the above financing methods are assumed to be covered by private capital, including infrastructure funds, private equity funds, private credit, real estate funds, etc.

Demonstration calculations, not actual predictions. Source: Listed company announcements, Bloomberg, CICC Research Department. AI Infrastructure Capital Expenditure: From Cash Flow to Debt. A New Cycle of AI Capital Expenditure Creates Financing Demand. Market predictions for AI capital expenditure over the next five years are mostly between $4 trillion and $8 trillion. There are significant differences in the calculation methods and logic. We selected the market consensus expectation of approximately $5.6 trillion as the assumption for AI capital expenditure from 2026 to 2030 to provide a basis for subsequent calculations of the financing gap. Specifically, we used the capital expenditure of five major cloud vendors [1] in 2025 as the base [2] and used the market consensus expectation of AI-related revenue of NVIDIA and AMD [3] as a proxy indicator to deduce the future investment growth rate of the AI ​​industry chain. The market consensus forecasts a 5-year CAGR of approximately 44% for AI-related revenue for NVIDIA and AMD, significantly higher than the market consensus forecast of around 21% growth in capital expenditures supported by cloud vendors' operating cash flow by the end of 2025. This difference suggests that relying solely on cloud vendors' operating cash flow may be insufficient to support AI capital expenditures, necessitating greater external financing in the future. Chart 2: Chip manufacturers' AI-related revenue growth rate is higher than that of cloud vendors' capital expenditure growth rate. [Image of chart 2: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7493954_image3.png] Note: Data for 2026-2030 is based on Bloomberg consensus estimates; this is a demonstrative calculation and does not represent actual forecasts. Source: Listed company announcements, Bloomberg, CICC Research Department. Large cloud vendors: From cash flow to debt. As of the end of 2025, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, and Oracle had combined assets of approximately $2.6 trillion, a debt-to-equity ratio of approximately 45%, and a net debt/EBITDA ratio of only about 0.3. These five companies collectively generated approximately $580 billion in operating cash flow annually. However, under pressure from AI capital expenditures, some cloud vendors have seen their leverage ratios rise. The ratio of capital expenditures to revenue for these five companies increased from approximately 12% in 2023 to approximately 23% in 2025, and according to market consensus, this ratio may further rise to over 40% by 2027, approaching or even exceeding the historical highs of capital expenditure cycles in the internet and energy industries. The market expects the combined free cash flow of major cloud vendors to decrease from approximately $200 billion in 2025 to approximately $24 billion in 2027, correspondingly reducing funds available for stock buybacks, dividends, and other purposes. Therefore, the AI ​​investments of major cloud vendors may shift from operating cash flow to greater reliance on external financing. Chart 3: AI vendors benefit from cloud vendors' capital expenditures, but declining free cash flow for cloud vendors puts pressure on stock prices. [Image of chart 3: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7493955_image3.png] Note: 2026-2027 forecasts use Bloomberg consensus estimates; the AI ​​vendor stock price index includes NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, SK Hynix, Samsung Electronics, Micron Technology, and ASML; the AI ​​cloud vendor stock price index includes Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Oracle.
Source: Factset, Bloomberg, Listed Company Announcements, CICC Research Department

Chart 4: Cloud Vendors' Capital Expenditure Intensity Rises, Expected to Surpass the Dot-com Bubble and Energy Bubble

Note: 2026-2028 uses Bloomberg consensus forecasts


Source: Factset, Bloomberg, Listed Company Announcements, CICC Research Department

$3.5 Trillion: The Financing Gap for AI Capital Expenditure

Chart 5: AI Capital Expenditure Creates a $3.5 Trillion Financing Gap

Note: This is a demonstrative calculation and does not represent actual predictions.

... The scale of AI capital expenditure in the industry is calculated backwards from the AI-related revenue growth rates of Nvidia and AMD and the capital expenditure of major cloud vendors in 2025; capital expenditure supported by operating cash flow is estimated based on the Bloomberg consensus forecast of capital expenditure of major cloud vendors at the end of 2025, taking into account the capital expenditure space beyond the needs of dividends and share buybacks within operating cash flow; Source: Listed company announcements, Bloomberg, CICC Research Department Where will the $3.5 trillion come from: AI financing structure Investment-grade bonds: the ballast stone of AI financing We estimate that investment-grade bonds can provide approximately $1.5 trillion in funding for AI investment between 2026 and 2030, accounting for approximately 42% of external financing needs, making it the largest single financing channel. Major cloud vendors have strong operating cash flow, low initial leverage, and investment-grade credit ratings, enabling them to obtain large-scale, long-term, and relatively low-cost funding in the global bond market. Based on rating agency leverage constraints, bond index concentration, and target debt/EBITDA, we estimate the issuance capacity to be approximately $1.2 trillion to $2.3 trillion. Since the beginning of 2026, the bond issuance scale of the five major cloud vendors has reached approximately $190 billion, a record high. The issuance currency has also expanded from the US dollar to the euro, pound sterling, and Canadian dollar. This diversification of bond financing can alleviate issuance pressure. The investment-grade bond market is capable of absorbing the majority of AI financing, but the continued large-scale, long-term bond supply may also increase the concentration of the technology industry in credit indices, leading to problems such as widening long-term credit spreads, rising financing costs, and strengthened rating constraints. Chart 6: Large Cloud Vendors' Bond Issuance Reaches Record High, with Diversified Currencies. Note: As of July 14, 2026. Source: Bloomberg, CICC Research Department. Chart 7: Calculation of the Upper Limit for Investment-Grade Bond Issuance by Cloud Vendors. Note: Data as of July 14, 2026.
Source: Bloomberg, S&P Global, CICC Research Department

Chart 8: AI Ultra-Long-Term Bond Issuance May Lead to Widening of Long-Term Credit Spreads

Source: Wind, CICC Research Department

Leveraged Financing: Transitional Capital for High-Growth Companies

Based on the market size ratio of leveraged financing and investment-grade bonds (approximately 20%), we estimate that high-yield bonds and leveraged loans could provide approximately $0.3 trillion in funding for AI investments, accounting for approximately 8% of external financing needs. This market primarily serves Neocloud, HPC operators, and data center companies that have not yet obtained investment-grade ratings but possess a certain revenue and asset base.

Chart 9: The US leveraged financing market is worth approximately $3 trillion. [Image of chart 9: Data as of July 14, 2026] [Source: Bloomberg, CICC Research Department] Public Market Equity: From “De-equityization” to “Re-equityization” Assuming a 20% equity ratio for projects (the industry average is typically 10%-30%), with 50% provided by public equity, we estimate that public equity could provide approximately $0.4 trillion in funding for AI investments between 2026 and 2030. Equity capital does not require fixed repayment of principal and interest, and can initially bear the risks of technological iteration, commercialization, and asset residual value. Therefore, it is more suitable for AI model companies, Neocloud, and data center operators that have not yet formed stable cash flow. Over the past decade, US-listed companies have tended to repurchase shares and increase dividends, thereby reducing outstanding shares and increasing earnings per share. This has weakened the equity market's function as a financing channel, a phenomenon known as "de-equityization." "Re-equityization" signifies a reversal of this trend, with companies relying more heavily on new issuances to raise funds. Equity financing is highly dependent on market valuation levels and issuance windows; once market volatility increases or valuations decline, the pace of financing may slow significantly. Chart 10: US TMT Equity Financing Scale Expected to Reach a Record High This Year. [Image of chart 10: Data as of July 14, 2026] Note: Includes US IPOs, private placements, public offerings, rights issues, etc.; data as of July 14, 2026. Source: Bloomberg, CICC Research Department. Securitization Products: From Corporate Credit to Asset Credit. Assuming that AI financing through securitization products will double in 26-30 years compared to 25-year levels, we estimate that ABS, CMBS, and other asset-backed financing could provide approximately $0.3 trillion in funding between 2026 and 2030, accounting for about 9% of external financing needs. Securitization financing is primarily applicable to data center and computing power assets that are already operational, have long-term leases, or identifiable equipment cash flows, shifting the financing basis from overall corporate credit to specific assets, leases, and cash flows. Securitization helps unlock construction loans and private equity capital, and provides long-term assets for insurance, pension, and fixed-income funds, but its expansion still depends on lease stability, tenant credit, asset residual value, and standardization. Especially for devices with rapid technological iterations, such as GPUs, the rate of depreciation and the value in the secondary market may become important constraints on rating and structural design. Chart 11: US Data Center Securitization Products to Raise Approximately $30 Billion in 2025

Note: Data as of July 14, 2026

Source: Bloomberg, CICC Research Department

Private Equity: A Flexible Supplement to the Financing Gap

We estimate that private equity (including infrastructure funds, private equity, private credit, and real estate funds) can absorb approximately $1.1 trillion in funding, accounting for about 30% of external financing needs, second only to investment-grade bonds.

We estimate that private equity (including infrastructure funds, private equity, private credit, and real estate funds, etc.) can absorb approximately $1.1 trillion in funding, accounting for about 30% of external financing needs, second only to investment-grade bonds.

The advantage of private equity capital lies in its flexible structure, allowing for the combination of equity, preferred stock, mezzanine financing, and project loans to mitigate construction period and non-standardized risks that are difficult to price in the public market. Private equity funds can also establish project joint ventures (JVs) or special purpose vehicles (SPVs) with cloud vendors, introducing equity and debt financing at the project level. Subject to accounting conditions, such arrangements can create off-balance-sheet financing, reducing the cloud vendors' upfront capital investment and consolidation burden, while simultaneously diversifying risk to external capital. However, the supply of private equity capital is subject to certain liability-side constraints. According to data from Preqin and McKinsey, in the 12 months ending June 2025, the cash distribution ratio of private equity funds to AUM was approximately 6%, a recent low; redemption pressure on some private equity credit semi-liquid products also increased further in 2026. Therefore, private equity capital is expected to become an important source of incremental AI financing, but its actual absorption capacity still depends on fund raising and product liquidity. Chart 12: Data Center Investment Rises Significantly Chart 13: Private Equity Fundraising Declines, But Infrastructure Funds Grow Rapidly Chart 14: Private Equity Fundraising Declines, But Infrastructure Funds Grow Rapidly Source: Preqin, McKinsey, CICC Research Department Chart 14: Private Equity Funds' Cash Distribution Ratio Hits a New Low in Recent Years

Source: Preqin, McKinsey, CICC Research Department

A Trillion-Dollar Question: How to Repay AI Debt?

1 Trillion Dollars: How Much Revenue is Needed for AI Financing to "Break Even"?

Under the baseline scenario, we estimate that to meet debt repayment requirements and shareholder returns, under the assumption of a 10% ROIC, AI applications ultimately need to generate approximately 1 trillion US dollars (958 billion US dollars in the baseline scenario) of sustainable commercial revenue annually.

Our calculations take into account assumptions such as leverage ratios, the economic lifespan of infrastructure, and profit margins in the mature stage. We assume that approximately 70% of the economic value is reflected in application revenue, with the remaining 30% coming from cost savings and efficiency improvements for enterprises. Market estimates for the long-term revenue required for AI range from $600 billion to $2 trillion, but these estimates differ significantly in their assumptions, logic, and definitions. Our $1 trillion estimate is in the middle of the market forecast range, but it still implies that AI commercialization needs to achieve orders-of-magnitude expansion in the coming years: assuming this revenue scale is reached by 2030, it means that AI application revenue needs to nearly double annually over the next five years. Chart 15: AI Financing Return on Capital Calculation [Image of chart 15: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7493967_image3.png] Note: This is for demonstration purposes only and does not represent actual predictions. The AI ​​large-scale model industry revenue is assumed to be 90% of the combined revenue of OpenAI, Anthropic, Zhipu, and Minimax. The project's debt-to-equity ratio is assumed to be 85%. ROIC is the pre-tax return on capital. Profit margins already account for maintenance expenses. Source: Listed company announcements, IMF, Counterpoint Research, CICC Research Department. A trillion US dollars annually is equivalent to 0.7% of global GDP, or $46 per iPhone per month. However, AI commercialization is not simply about individual payments; revenue can come from various channels such as corporate spending, advertising, e-commerce, and government procurement. According to Gartner, global SaaS end-user spending is projected to reach $300 billion in 2025, and global public cloud service spending is projected to reach $700 billion. In comparison, a trillion US dollars in annual AI revenue is approximately 3.2 times the global SaaS market and 1.4 times the global public cloud service market in 2025. Therefore, from an industry scale perspective, a trillion US dollars may be a challenging but not unrealistic revenue target, and debt repayment and shareholder returns for AI financing are not unattainable. Sensitivity Analysis of Key Assumptions The assumptions regarding profit margin and depreciation period in the above calculations have significant uncertainties. Based on a baseline scenario, we assume an EBITDA profit margin of approximately 50% at the maturity stage of the AI ​​business; however, whether this level can be achieved currently lacks sufficient financial data support. Major large-scale model companies are still in the investment phase, with some companies experiencing adjusted net loss rates of 300%-500%. Another key variable is the depreciation period of infrastructure. Data center buildings and infrastructure typically have a lifespan of over ten years, while chips and electronic equipment face faster technological iterations, potentially leading to faster depreciation of older equipment and a shorter overall depreciation period for data centers. Therefore, we conducted a sensitivity analysis on the two variables mentioned above, and the results show that: 1. With a depreciation period of approximately 7 years, if the EBITDA profit margin reaches approximately 30%, the return on capital can be close to the break-even point; if the profit margin is below 20%, even with a longer asset lifespan, it will be difficult to cover the cost of capital. 2. Under the assumption of an EBITDA profit margin of approximately 50%, a positive return on investment can be achieved as long as the depreciation period reaches approximately 5 years; if the asset lifespan is further extended, the rate of return will significantly increase. Therefore, judging whether AI investment can "break even" cannot only be based on revenue scale, but more importantly, factors such as profit margin and depreciation. Lower-than-expected profit margins or premature depreciation of equipment will significantly raise the required revenue threshold. Chart 16: AI investment returns vary significantly under different profit margins and infrastructure economic lives.

Note: Calculated based on AI application revenue under the baseline scenario (approximately $1 trillion annually).
Source: CICC Research Department

Chart 17: Some AI large-scale model companies are still in a high-loss stage

Note: Adjusted net loss is the non-IFRS indicator disclosed by the company, mainly excluding share-based payments, changes in the fair or book value of pre-listing financial instruments, and listing expenses.

Note: Adjusted net loss is the non-IFRS indicator disclosed by the company, mainly excluding share-based payments, changes in the fair or book value of pre-listing financial instruments, and listing expenses.

Source: Listed company announcements, Bloomberg, CICC Research Department. Opportunities and Risks for Financial Institutions. AI Investment Drives Bank Balance Sheet Expansion. Data center projects lack stable cash flow during the construction phase, typically requiring bank loans. After the project is operational and leases are established, the cash flow is replaced by long-term bonds, private equity loans, and other products. Banks therefore play a more significant bridging role between the construction phase and the capital market. Financing to non-bank financial institutions is also driving bank credit expansion. Banks can provide leverage to non-bank institutions through fund subscription financing, NAV loans, etc. Private equity capital bears the long-term risk of the underlying projects, while banks provide relatively shorter-term, higher-priority financing. As of June 2026, US bank loans to non-bank financial institutions increased by approximately 25% year-on-year, significantly faster than general loans. The related growth reflects the driving effect of private equity investment on bank lending, with AI infrastructure being a significant investment area.

Chart 18: Non-bank Loans Drive the Upward Trend of the US Credit Cycle

Note: The impact of statistical adjustments on non-bank loans, industrial and commercial loans, and consumer loans has been removed.

Source: Federal Reserve, Wind, CICC Research Department

AI Financing Brings Capital Market Revenue

The more direct impact of AI financing on banks is reflected in the growth of non-interest income. In Q2 2026, the combined net profit of the six largest US banks increased by 40% year-on-year, and operating income increased by 22% year-on-year, of which non-interest income increased by 32% year-on-year.

The increase in corporate IPOs, mergers and acquisitions, and bond issuance driven by AI financing is a significant driver of related revenue growth. Large, diversified banks possess stronger capital market service capabilities and can generate fee income through AI financing while reducing the occupancy of their long-term balance sheets. In contrast, small and medium-sized banks can obtain higher interest rate spreads through project loans, but they also bear higher capital pressure and credit risk. It is worth noting that bank revenue is often recognized during the financing stage, while credit risk only becomes apparent during the project repayment stage, exhibiting a characteristic of "revenue upfront, risk deferred." Chart 19: The current upward cycle in US banking performance is driven by capital market-related revenue. [Image of chart 19: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7493971_image3.png] Note: The six largest US banks include: JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, Citigroup, Wells Fargo, Goldman Sachs, and Morgan Stanley. Source: Listed company announcements, Bloomberg, CICC Research Department. From AI Risk to Financial Risk: Can Refinancing Replace Cash Flow? Large cloud vendors typically issue long-term bonds with maturities of 10-30 years, and infrastructure funds often have investment periods of over 10 years. After data centers are operational, they can also use project bonds and securitization to replace construction period financing. These arrangements reduce short-term debt repayment pressure and allow time for AI commercialization. If revenue realization is slower than expected, companies can also extend debt maturities through refinancing. However, refinancing only buys time and cannot replace cash flow: if project utilization, profit margins, and asset lifespan are consistently lower than expected, continuous rolling financing will only increase leverage and financing costs. Our calculations indicate that large cloud vendors' bonds will reach their peak maturity period between 2027 and 2032, with an annual maturity scale of approximately $28 billion, a 60% increase compared to 2024-2026, leading to increased refinancing pressure. Chart 20: Major Cloud Vendors' Bond Maturities Peak Starting in 2027

Source: Bloomberg, CICC Research Department

Cloud vendor credit is a core risk anchor. Major cloud vendors are the main bearers of AI capital expenditures and are also the ultimate counterparties for bond issuers, data center tenants, Neocloud customers, and project SPVs. Their creditworthiness determines whether most of the industry chain's cash flow can be sustained. Cloud vendor CDS spreads can serve as a high-frequency indicator of credit risk. Since the end of 2025, CDS spreads of major cloud vendors have generally widened, but significant differentiation has emerged among different entities.

As of July 2026, the 5-year CDS spreads for Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta remained roughly between 45 and 80 basis points, while Oracle and CoreWeave spreads were above 180 and 600 basis points, respectively. This indicates that the market is focusing on pricing the risks of entities with high leverage, weak free cash flow, and high reliance on refinancing. [Chart 21: Rising Spreads for Pricing Debt Default Risk of AI Cloud Vendors] [Source: Bloomberg, CICC Research Department] Financial Risk Transmission Path: If AI commercialization falls short of expectations, the risk will first manifest as a decline in data center utilization, revenue, and profit margins, further depressing asset residual value. Losses are typically borne first by cloud vendors, Neocloud, and shareholders of the project's SPV, and reflected in the stock market through market capitalization revaluation. Only if operating cash flow continues to deteriorate and depletes the equity buffer will the risk further transmit to corporate bonds, leveraged financing, securitized products, and private lending. Bank loans usually have a higher priority and are therefore generally at the back end of the loss absorption chain. Is AI a "bubble"? A perspective from the financial system. The scale of financing itself does not equate to a bubble; the key lies in whether the new capital can generate sufficiently stable final demand and cash flow. The capital market can solve the problem of "whether AI infrastructure can be built," but it cannot solve the problem of "whether the investment can be recovered." We estimate that related investments will ultimately need to support approximately $1 trillion in annual AI application revenue. This goal is not unattainable, but it places high demands on capacity utilization, profit margins, and the economic life of equipment. If commercial growth continues to lag behind capital expenditure, the risk will gradually shift from valuation corrections to overcapacity, declining asset residual value, and deteriorating credit quality, and will be transmitted through the financial system via revolving financing, private lending, and securitization. The scale of financing itself is not the problem; the real test is whether a cash flow cycle can be formed before financing costs rise and assets depreciate.

Chart 22: The Transmission Chain of AI Financial Risk

Source: CICC Research Department

data.title: SEC pushes for 24/7 trading in US stocks – will RWA’s biggest advantage disappear?
data.description:
By Mahe, Foresight News

On July 23, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) announced that it will hold a public roundtable meeting at its Washington headquarters on September 17, focusing on preparations for the U.S. stock market to move towards 24-hour trading. The meeting will discuss the infrastructure supporting overnight trading, operations and resilience in a 24-hour market, and the opportunities and challenges of extending trading hours.

SEC Chairman Paul S. Atkins stated in a statement: "We are heading towards a new day—and a new night—for the U.S. stock market. With the expansion of overnight trading, I look forward to the U.S. stock market aligning with those markets that have already achieved continuous trading, while also looking forward to striking a balance between 24/7 trading and the crucial protection of investors and clients."

This roundtable discussion may signify a crucial juncture in the substantial transition of U.S. stocks from traditional trading hours to a near-23x5 (23x5) model. For the crypto industry, especially in the RWA (Real-World Asset) tokenization field, this change both compresses some of the differentiation narratives and opens up deeper space for integration.

US Stock Trading Embraces Change Since 1985, the regular trading hours for exchanges such as the New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq have been fixed at 9:30 a.m. to 4:00 p.m. Eastern Time, Monday through Friday. This arrangement has continued for over four decades. While maintaining regular trading hours for US stocks has been beneficial for centralized price discovery and reduced operating costs, in today's highly electronic global capital flows, this arrangement is outdated. Asian and European investors who want to trade US stocks during their local daytime trading hours often have to rely on futures or illiquid pre- and after-hours trading, a far less convenient experience than trading crypto assets. Meanwhile, retail investors have become accustomed to placing orders anytime via mobile apps and are increasingly impatient with the "waiting" that prevents them from trading immediately after major news releases. Outside of regular hours, there are limited extended trading windows. Pre-market trading typically runs from midnight to 9:30 AM; after-hours trading extends from 4 PM to around 8 PM. These extended sessions are offered by exchanges, alternative trading systems (ATS), and brokerage platforms, but liquidity is significantly lower than regular sessions, spreads are wider, and price discovery efficiency is lower. Regulators such as FINRA have repeatedly warned investors of the higher volatility and execution risks associated with extended sessions. In recent years, some brokerages and ATSs have further extended trading into overnight sessions. For example, platforms like Interactive Brokers offer overnight trading from 8 PM Sunday to approximately 3:50 AM Friday (with a brief maintenance window), covering some S&P 500, Nasdaq 100 constituent stocks, and ETFs. However, according to observations of the public market, actual overnight trading volume has consistently accounted for only a very small percentage of the total market volume, making it difficult to generate deep price discovery. Overall, the US stock market remains characterized by concentrated daytime trading on weekdays, with complete market closures on weekends and most holidays. This contrasts sharply with the 24/7, year-round operation of the crypto asset market, and is one of the key differentiators frequently emphasized in the RWA tokenization narrative. 23/5 Trading? Since 2024, several exchanges and market infrastructure institutions have accelerated their efforts towards near-24/7 trading. 24X National Exchange is the first national securities exchange to receive SEC approval to conduct 23-hour trading (23x5), and plans to officially launch full 23/5 night trading in the second half of 2026. NYSE Arca plans to extend its trading hours to approximately 22 hours, also aiming for the end of 2026. Nasdaq has publicly proposed the "Global Trading Hours" plan, aiming to add a night trading session from 9 PM to 4 AM the following day, creating a nearly 23-hour trading day when combined with existing sessions. The target timeframe is also the second half of 2026, provided regulatory approval and industry infrastructure align. Cboe is also advancing its 23x5 plan for platforms like EDGX. Infrastructure progress is even more crucial. DTCC's National Securities Clearing Corporation (NSCC) launched its 24x5 clearing service on June 28, 2026, covering trading activity from 8 PM Sunday to 8 PM Friday, providing immediate central counterparty guarantees for overnight trades, significantly reducing counterparty risk. These developments mean that by the end of 2026 or early 2027, US stocks are expected to achieve near-continuous weekday trading within the regulatory framework. True 24/7 (including weekends) trading still faces higher hurdles, but the "money never sleeps" concept has moved from discussion to implementation. The roundtable meeting on September 17th was a public alignment between the SEC and representatives from exchanges, market makers, clearinghouses, brokerages, and investors on core issues such as operational resilience, liquidity management, and investor protection, prior to the official large-scale opening of trading. The move towards near-24/7 trading in US stocks directly impacted one of the most frequently used selling points of RWA tokenization platforms—"Traditional markets only allow daytime trading, while on-chain trading is available 24/7." The current RWA market has reached a certain scale, especially in perpetual contract trading volume. According to the latest data from Blockworks, last week, Hyperliquid's RWA trading volume surpassed crypto asset trading for the first time, accounting for 54% of the platform's total trading volume. Among these, single-stock trading has become the fastest-growing category. Since June, individual stock trading volume has exceeded that of indices and commodities, currently accounting for approximately 61% of Hyperliquid RWA's total trading volume, indicating that the on-chain financial market is expanding from purely crypto asset trading to traditional assets. With the extension of trading hours for US stocks, the appeal of simply "being able to trade stocks during Asian hours or at night" will be diluted. Institutional and cross-border investors will be able to complete near-24/7 transactions more directly on regulated traditional exchanges and ATS, without having to rely entirely on tokenized channels. In terms of compliance costs, custody, and settlement certainty, traditional paths remain more attractive to many large funds. However, the impact is not entirely negative. True 24/7 and instant atomic settlement remain on-chain advantages. Even with 23x5 trading hours for US stocks, weekend and holiday gaps still exist, and clearing and settlement still rely on the DTCC system, making it difficult to completely eliminate T+0 and T+1 friction. RWA tokens can achieve near-instant ownership transfer and settlement on public chains and naturally support programmable logic, including automatic dividends, staking, and portfolio management. The regulatory environment is evolving in tandem. The SEC, under Paul Atkins' leadership, has explicitly included "facilitating on-chain trading of tokenized securities" in its 2026 regulatory agenda and is advancing a token classification framework. Tokenized securities are still defined as securities and subject to federal securities laws, but innovation exemptions and related rule adjustments are under discussion. The DTCC is also advancing tokenized settlement pilots. The experience accumulated in overnight liquidity management, price protection, and system resilience during the extended trading hours of US stocks actually provides a reference for regulatory practices in the compliant RWA secondary market. Some crypto platforms have explored cooperation, such as integrating tokenized US stocks with traditional account systems and global liquidity. On July 10th of this year, Backpack launched the world's first 24/7 real US stock trading market for international investors. Users can buy, sell, and hold real US stocks around the clock, rather than synthetic derivatives. Ondo's tokenized stocks are backed by 1:1 underlying securities and are pegged to traditional market liquidity through a minting and redemption mechanism. Users can trade these tokens directly on cryptocurrency exchanges, while non-US users can more easily gain exposure to US stocks. For the RWA platform, the real competitive advantage is shifting from "trading time differences" to "settlement finality, cross-border accessibility, DeFi composability, and interoperability with traditional infrastructure." Tokenization of non-public market assets such as private lending, real estate, and alternative assets is less affected by changes in US stock trading hours and may even benefit from increased overall tokenization acceptance. However, risks also need to be acknowledged. Overnight liquidity is inevitably thin initially, making prices more susceptible to manipulation. If the RWA platform opens trading during a low-liquidity window, it must simultaneously strengthen risk control, circuit breakers, and market-making mechanisms; otherwise, it may amplify volatility and damage the industry's reputation. Regulatory pressure on purely licensed and synthetic products may further increase as traditional markets extend trading hours, correspondingly enhancing the value of compliant issuance and ATS/exchange pathways.

data.title: Google's financial report: cloud profit margins, TPU accounting treatment, SpaceX computing power leases
data.description:
Author: Zhao Ying, Zhuifeng Trading Platform

Alphabet's Q2 earnings report revealed key signals regarding the return on investment in generative AI, but higher capital expenditures are depressing earnings per share expectations. Following the earnings report, Morgan Stanley lowered its target price for Alphabet from $415 to $400, maintaining an "overweight" rating, believing the current share price still has approximately 20% upside potential.

Google Cloud exceeded expectations with a year-over-year growth rate of 82%, adding $4.7 billion in quarterly revenue and reaching a backlog of $514 billion; simultaneously, the company raised its 2026 capital expenditure cap by $15 billion to $205 billion.

According to Zhuifeng Trading Platform, Morgan Stanley, in its July 23 report, expects capital expenditures to further climb to $375 billion in 2027.

This massive investment prompted Morgan Stanley to lower its 2027 and 2028 earnings per share forecasts by 7% and 5%, respectively, with the core logic being that investment growth continues to outpace revenue growth. In terms of market impact, analyst Brian Nowak believes that Google Cloud's rapid growth and profit margin performance are the most direct benchmarks for the market to assess the return on investment in generative AI. The approximately $50 per watt computing power leasing agreement between Alphabet and SpaceX (SPCX) provides a new perspective on understanding the company's recent capacity shortfall. Google Cloud: Growth and Profit Margin Exceed Expectations, Order Backlog Provides Visibility. Google Cloud's second-quarter year-on-year growth reached 82%, exceeding Morgan Stanley's 77% forecast, with a quarter-on-quarter increase in revenue of $4.7 billion. Profit margins were also strong: Q2 EBIT margin was 36%, slightly higher than Morgan Stanley's 35% forecast; incremental margin was 54%, similar to Q1's 57%, indicating that scale expansion did not significantly erode earnings quality. Order backlog data further reinforces the sustainability of growth. Google Cloud's current order backlog is $514 billion, an increase of $52 billion quarter-over-quarter, 7% higher than forecast. This backlog provides strong visibility for revenue growth over the next few years. The report also points out that Google Cloud's rapid growth puts tactical pressure on competitor AWS. However, the report also notes that AWS's revenue is heavily reliant on API-driven revenue (recorded on a net basis), and the accounting treatment of TPU sales differs, introducing some noise into direct comparisons between the two. Capital Expenditure: Growth Logic Driven by Accelerated Capacity Delivery, Not Cost Inflation. Alphabet has raised its 2026 capital expenditure cap by $15 billion to $205 billion. This increase is driven by the accelerated pace of computing power delivery, rather than rising component prices—a crucial distinction for assessing investment efficiency. Looking ahead, Alphabet's capital expenditure is projected to rise to $375 billion in 2027 and further to $400 billion in 2028, primarily to prepare for the large-scale deployment of TPU and GPU capacity in 2028. Correspondingly, the 2026 and 2027 capital expenditure forecasts have been revised upwards by 8% and 7%, respectively. Regarding financing arrangements, the model assumes that Alphabet will raise approximately $80 billion in new debt financing by the end of 2027 to support the aforementioned investment scale. Management's statements are also noteworthy—the company stated that compared to a year ago, it is now more optimistic about the opportunities for generative AI in both enterprise and consumer sectors, which corroborates its continued increase in capital expenditure. TPU Accounting Treatment: Inventory Building, Gross Profit Recognition, and Revenue Flow Confirmation This financial report provides important confirmation of the accounting treatment logic for TPUs. According to the report, Alphabet purchases TPUs in the form of system components (including racks, etc.) and holds them as inventory for a period of time; total TPU sales are included in backlog orders, and revenue is recognized through Google Cloud when sold externally. Alphabet uses a physical sales model with gross profit, rather than a technology licensing model. A small amount of third-party TPU sales revenue was already included in the second quarter. Approximately 3.5GW of TPU sales will be concentrated in 2027, with another approximately 4GW expected to be realized in 2028. Regarding pricing assumptions, the benchmark model assumes a price of $20 per watt for each GW TPU rack, corresponding to a gross margin of approximately 20%, but analysts admit this assumption is still under validation. The market currently underestimates the TPU opportunity in 2028, and investors may be underestimating both the required capital expenditure and the potential upside from managed cloud and third-party TPU sales. SpaceX Computing Power Lease: $50 per watt for "Hyperscale Cloud Customers," Apple May Be One Source of Demand. The computing power lease agreement between Alphabet and SpaceX (SPCX) has been previously disclosed, and this earnings report provides further details. The agreement is priced at approximately $50 per watt for serving "hyperscale cloud customers," and management stated that despite the higher short-term lease costs, the lifetime value of the customer relationship "has a highly positive return on investment." Combining the upcoming launch of the Gemini-powered Siri AI feature this fall with the continued training of new models by private lab customers on the Google Cloud platform, it's reasonable to infer that multiple factors are driving Alphabet's recent surge in computing power demand. Regarding the financial impact, the report incorporates the agreement into its model, projecting approximately $1 billion and $3 billion in additional operating expenses in the third and fourth quarters, respectively, based on the September launch date and a monthly rate of $920 million; and an additional $3 billion in the first quarter of 2027. It also assumes that Alphabet will choose to terminate the contract at the earliest feasible time—the contract terms allow either party to terminate with 90 days' notice after December 31, 2026. Management's characterization of it as a "six-month agreement" confirms the company's intention to exit once its own computing power capacity is available. Valuation and Rating: Target Price $400, Two Catalysts Determine Excess Return Potential. Morgan Stanley lowered its target price from $415 to $400, corresponding to a P/E ratio of approximately 24 times the average EPS of $15/$18 in 2027/2028, representing a premium of approximately 35% over the industry median. The current share price of $342.09 implies an upside of approximately 20% from the target price. The report lists two key catalysts: First, the release schedule of the Gemini 4 series models (including the high-efficiency, low-cost version), with management stating that it is committed to increasing the model iteration frequency, aiming to achieve monthly updates in the Gemini 4 phase; Second, improved visibility of the TPU opportunity in 2028, including further clarification of revenue scale and profit margin. Regarding risk scenarios, the bull market target price is $450 (corresponding to approximately a P/E ratio of 26), and the bear market target price is $225 (corresponding to approximately a P/E ratio of 15). Implied probabilities in the options market indicate that the probability of the stock price exceeding $400 in the next 12 months is approximately 28.1%, and the probability of exceeding $450 is approximately 17.1%.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase Research Report: Google Cloud Revenue Surges 82%, Recommends Buying on Dips
data.description:
Author: Rita, Deep Tide

Google's Q2 earnings report revealed two core themes: accelerated commercialization of AI and a further upward revision of capital expenditure expectations.

Cloud revenue grew 82% year-over-year, search revenue grew 17% year-over-year, and TPUs contributed over $1 billion in revenue for the first time in a single quarter. AI-driven momentum has permeated all of Google's business lines. The company revised its 2026 capital expenditure guidance upward from the $180 billion-$190 billion range to the $195 billion-$205 billion range, and indicated that capital expenditure will continue to grow significantly in 2027. JPMorgan Chase predicts that capital expenditure will reach $378 billion in 2027, an 85% year-over-year increase.

JPMorgan maintains its overweight rating on Google with a target price of $420, corresponding to a P/E ratio of 25 times its 2028 GAAP EPS of $16.66. The firm recommends buying on dips. The institution believes that AI investment has entered a period of return on investment, and the upward revision of capital expenditure is demand-driven, while the pressure on profit margins is a temporary phenomenon. If the market sells off due to concerns about the scale of capital expenditure, it will present a good entry point. Cloud business growth continues to exceed expectations, and AI commercialization is accelerating. Google Cloud achieved revenue of $24.77 billion in the second quarter, a year-on-year increase of 82%, with the growth rate continuing to rise. The order backlog at the end of the period reached $514 billion, an increase of $52 billion quarter-on-quarter, of which approximately 50% is expected to be recognized as revenue in the next 24 months. New customer acquisition doubled year-over-year, and existing customer resource consumption exceeded committed usage by more than 50%. Gemini API processing capacity increased from approximately 16 billion tokens per minute in Q1 to 22 billion tokens per minute, a 37.5% increase quarter-over-quarter. Approximately 90% of Fortune 100 companies have adopted Gemini Enterprise, with over 9 million monthly active developers. TPU revenue was recognized independently for the first time in Q2, with JPMorgan Chase estimating quarterly revenue exceeding $1 billion. Google uses a direct sales model, not a licensing model, and revenue is recognized grossly. Most TPU orders are currently included in the backlog and are expected to be delivered in 2027, with TPU revenue continuing to be released in 2026. Search business showed stronger resilience than expected, with AI driving incremental queries. The market had previously been concerned that AI would divert search traffic, but Q2 data showed the opposite trend. Search and other business revenue grew by 17% year-over-year, with major vertical industries such as retail, finance, and technology all contributing positively. Monthly active users of AI features surpassed 1 billion, continuing to contribute incremental search requests. AI optimization improved the relevance of shopping ads by 20%, and approximately 500,000 advertisers have adopted AI Max tools, driving billions of new monetizable queries. YouTube ad revenue grew by 13% year-over-year, driven by both brand and performance advertising, with FIFA World Cup-related advertising also contributing. Capital Expenditure Revised Upward Again, Driven by Demand Rather Than Cost Pressures. The company has revised its 2026 capital expenditure guidance upward from the $180 billion-$190 billion range to the $195 billion-$205 billion range, an increase of approximately $15 billion. JPMorgan Chase points out that this upward revision is mainly due to the company accelerating computing power delivery to match strong demand, rather than rising upstream costs. Capital expenditure is expected to continue to grow significantly in 2027, with the bank's forecast revised upward to $378 billion, representing an 85% year-on-year increase. Market concerns about the scale of capital expenditure are reasonable, but the funding structure needs attention. In the second quarter, the company completed approximately $20 billion in bond issuance and $85 billion in equity financing to support capital expenditure. Management stated that there are no plans to return to the equity market at this time, and the focus will be on operating cash flow combined with incremental bond issuance to maintain capital expenditures while keeping the balance sheet healthy. Profit margins are under short-term pressure, but the long-term recovery trend is clear. The operating profit margin in the second quarter was 34.0%, slightly lower than JPMorgan Chase's expectation of 34.4%. The pressure on profit margins mainly came from one-off and temporary factors: third-party computing power leasing to make up for the gap in self-built capacity; TPU hardware sales, where hardware gross margins are lower than software; Wiz integration costs; accelerated depreciation and legal fees, etc., none of which are structural deteriorations. The bank expects that as self-built capacity gradually comes online and the scale effect of TPU revenue becomes apparent, profit margins will steadily recover. The most crucial signal from this financial report is that Google's AI investment has moved from the "cash-burning period" to the "revenue realization period." Cloud business growth has continued to rise, TPUs have contributed revenue independently for the first time, and search business has not only escaped the diversion of revenue by AI but has accelerated its growth. Multiple data points confirm that the commercialization of AI is progressing faster than market expectations. The market still tends to evaluate Google using the traditional framework of "capital expenditure and profit margin," but JPMorgan believes this analytical framework needs updating. The upward revision of capital expenditure is demand-driven, with strong customer demand for computing power requiring the company to accelerate delivery. Pressure on profit margins is a temporary phenomenon, a transitional cost rather than a structural deterioration. If the market continues to price within the old framework, it may present an opportunity for investment. Google's financing strategy is also noteworthy. The combination of $20 billion in bond issuance and $85 billion in equity financing in the second quarter demonstrates management's determination to increase capital expenditure and indicates its unwillingness to excessively pressure its balance sheet. The $85 billion equity financing is relatively rare among tech giants, but JPMorgan judges this to be a one-off operation, with no further equity financing expected.
